name: Chettinad Chicken Curry
description: The crown jewel of Tamil Nadu's Chettinad cuisine — a fiercely aromatic curry built on freshly ground kalpasi, marathi mokku, and star anise. Deeply spiced, boldly flavored, and unlike anything else in Indian cooking.
instructions: Dry roast coriander seeds, black pepper, fennel seeds, star anise, cinnamon, cloves, and cardamom until fragrant, about 3 minutes.
Grind the roasted spices with grated coconut and dried red chilies into a coarse paste with a little water.
Heat oil in a heavy pan. Add curry leaves and sliced onions, frying until deep golden brown, about 15 minutes.
Add ginger-garlic paste and fry for 2 minutes until raw smell disappears.
Add chopped tomatoes and cook until they break down and oil separates, about 8 minutes.
Add the ground Chettinad spice paste and cook for 5 minutes, stirring constantly.
Add chicken pieces and coat well with the masala. Fry on high heat for 5 minutes to seal.
Add 1 cup water, reduce heat, cover and cook for 25 minutes until chicken is cooked through.
Uncover and simmer for 5 minutes to thicken the gravy. Adjust salt.
Garnish with fresh curry leaves and serve with appam or plain rice.
tips: True Chettinad masala uses kalpasi (stone flower) and marathi mokku — find them at South Indian grocery stores. The coconut is ground into the masala, not added at the end, which sets this apart from other South Indian curries.

name: Laal Maas (Rajasthani Red Mutton Curry)
description: Rajasthan's iconic fiery red mutton curry — cooked with Mathania dried red chilies and yogurt. A warrior's dish forged in the desert: bold, uncompromising, and deeply satisfying.
instructions: Soak dried red chilies in warm water for 30 minutes, then grind to a smooth paste with garlic and a little water.
Heat ghee and oil together in a heavy-bottomed pot. Add cumin seeds, cloves, cardamom, and bay leaves.
Add sliced onions and fry on medium-high heat until deep brown, about 20 minutes — this is the flavor base.
Add the red chili-garlic paste and cook for 5 minutes until oil separates.
Add mutton pieces and fry on high heat for 8 minutes until browned on all sides.
Add ginger paste, coriander powder, and salt. Mix well.
Lower heat and add whisked yogurt a spoonful at a time, stirring constantly to prevent curdling.
Add 1.5 cups warm water, bring to a boil, then cover and simmer for 60–75 minutes until mutton is fork-tender.
Uncover, increase heat, and cook until the gravy thickens and oil floats on top.
Serve with bajra roti or steamed rice, with raw onion rings and lemon wedges.
tips: The correct color and heat comes from Mathania or Kashmiri red chilies — do not use generic chili powder. Authentic Laal Maas is cooked in pure ghee, which carries the chili flavor beautifully through the meat.

name: Bisi Bele Bath
description: Karnataka's beloved one-pot comfort dish — 'hot lentil rice bath' — combining rice, toor dal, and vegetables in a deeply spiced tamarind-jaggery broth made with the signature Bisi Bele Bath powder.
instructions: Pressure cook rice and toor dal together with 4 cups water and turmeric for 4 whistles until soft and mushy.
Soak tamarind in 1 cup warm water for 15 minutes, then squeeze out the pulp.
Dry roast coriander seeds, chana dal, urad dal, dried red chilies, cinnamon, cloves, and coconut until fragrant. Grind to a powder — this is your Bisi Bele Bath masala.
In a large pot, heat 1 tbsp ghee and sauté onion. Add vegetables, tomatoes, and tamarind water.
Add jaggery, salt, turmeric, and the ground masala powder. Cook until vegetables are tender, about 15 minutes.
Add the mushy rice-dal mixture and stir to combine. Add water to adjust consistency — it should be thick like porridge.
Simmer together for 10 minutes for flavors to meld.
In a small pan, heat remaining ghee. Add mustard seeds and curry leaves until they splutter. Add asafoetida.
Pour the tempering over the dish and stir.
Serve hot with papad and a drizzle of extra ghee on top.
tips: Bisi Bele Bath should have a thick porridge consistency — not dry. The jaggery-tamarind balance is key: start with the stated amounts and adjust to your taste. Best eaten straight from the pot while steaming hot.

name: Tandoori Chicken
description: The original Punjabi clay-oven classic — chicken marinated overnight in yogurt and a vivid spice blend, then charred at intense heat until the outside is crisp and smoky while the inside stays juicy. Recreated at home under a broiler.

ingredient_quantities.chicken: 1 whole chicken, cut into 8 pieces, skin removed

instructions: Make deep cuts in the chicken pieces to allow the marinade to penetrate.
Rub chicken with lemon juice, salt, red chili powder, and half the turmeric. Rest 30 minutes.
Combine yogurt, ginger paste, garlic paste, Kashmiri chili powder, cumin, coriander, garam masala, remaining turmeric, and oil into a smooth marinade.
Coat chicken thoroughly, pushing marinade into the cuts. Marinate for at least 4 hours, overnight is best.
Preheat oven to highest setting (240°C/460°F) with a wire rack over a baking tray.
Place chicken on rack and roast 20 minutes. Flip, baste with butter, and broil 8–10 minutes until charred in spots.
Rest for 5 minutes.
Sprinkle with chaat masala and squeeze lemon juice over.
Serve with sliced onion rings, mint chutney, and naan.
tips: Use thick hung curd (yogurt drained of whey overnight) — watery yogurt won't coat the chicken. Place a baking tray at the bottom of the oven to catch drips. The charring is essential, not optional.

name: Malai Kofta
description: Soft paneer and potato dumplings stuffed with cashews and raisins, simmered in a velvety Mughal-style tomato-cream sauce perfumed with saffron and cardamom. The ultimate North Indian vegetarian celebration dish.

ingredient_quantities.saffron: a pinch, soaked in warm milk

instructions: Mix crumbled paneer, mashed potato, cornflour, and salt into a smooth dough. Divide into 12 portions.
Place a cashew and a few raisins in the center of each portion and shape into smooth balls.
Deep fry koftas in hot oil on medium heat until golden all over, about 4 minutes. Drain and set aside.
Fry onions with bay leaf, cinnamon, cardamom, and cloves in 2 tbsp oil until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook 2 minutes. Add tomatoes and cook until completely soft, 10 minutes.
Cool slightly, then blend to a smooth puree. Strain through a sieve for a silky texture.
Return to pan, add turmeric, chili powder, coriander powder, and salt. Cook 5 minutes.
Add cashew paste and milk, simmer 5 minutes. Add sugar to balance acidity.
Stir in cream and saffron-milk. Simmer on low for 5 minutes until rich and velvety.
Add koftas just before serving — do not boil them in the gravy. Drizzle cream and dust with garam masala.
tips: Add koftas to the gravy only just before serving — they absorb moisture and fall apart if left in too long. Straining the gravy is what gives it the restaurant-style silky finish.

name: Gajar Ka Halwa (Carrot Pudding)
description: North India's most beloved winter dessert — slow-cooked grated carrots in whole milk with ghee, cardamom, and sugar until jammy and luscious. Topped with saffron, pistachios, and khoya for a truly festive finish.

ingredient_quantities.saffron: a pinch, dissolved in warm milk

instructions: Heat 1 tbsp ghee in a wide, heavy-bottomed pan. Add grated carrots and sauté on medium heat for 5 minutes until the color deepens slightly.
Pour in milk and bring to a boil. Reduce heat and simmer uncovered, stirring frequently.
Cook until milk is fully absorbed, about 35–40 minutes, stirring every 5 minutes to prevent sticking.
Add sugar and stir — the mixture will loosen as sugar melts. Continue cooking until liquid evaporates again, about 10 minutes.
Add remaining ghee and fry the halwa for 5 minutes until it starts to pull away from the sides of the pan.
Add crumbled khoya, mix well, and cook 3 more minutes.
Add cardamom powder and saffron milk, stir through.
In a small pan, fry nuts and raisins in a little ghee until golden. Pour over halwa.
Serve warm or at room temperature.
tips: Red Delhi carrots make the best halwa — they are sweeter and have lower water content than orange varieties. Full-fat milk and generous ghee are non-negotiable. Khoya elevates it from good to extraordinary.

name: Kashmiri Dum Aloo
description: Small whole potatoes slow-braised in a vibrant yogurt and Kashmiri chili sauce scented with fennel and ginger powder. A signature of the Kashmir valley — bright red, mildly spiced, made without onion or garlic.
instructions: Prick parboiled potatoes all over with a fork to allow the sauce to penetrate.
Heat mustard oil to smoking point, then reduce to medium. Fry potatoes until golden brown on all sides, about 8 minutes. Remove.
In the same oil, add asafoetida, cumin seeds, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and bay leaves.
Mix Kashmiri chili powder and turmeric with 2 tbsp water into a paste. Add to oil and fry for 2 minutes.
Reduce heat to low. Add yogurt slowly, stirring constantly to prevent splitting.
Add fennel powder and dried ginger powder. Cook for 3 minutes.
Add ½ cup water, bring to a gentle simmer, then add fried potatoes.
Cover tightly and cook on very low heat (dum) for 20 minutes, shaking the pan occasionally.
Uncover and check gravy — it should coat the potatoes thickly. Adjust salt.
Serve with steamed rice or alongside Rogan Josh for a full Kashmiri meal.
tips: Authentic Kashmiri Dum Aloo uses no onion or garlic — do not add them. The two defining flavors are Kashmiri chili (color and mild heat) and fennel. Mustard oil is traditional and its sharpness is essential to the flavor.

name: Seekh Kebab
description: Mughal-heritage minced lamb skewers seasoned with whole spice powders and green herbs, charred over high heat until smoky outside and juicy within. A tandoor essential and one of India's most iconic kebabs.

ingredient_quantities.onion: 1 small, finely minced and squeezed dry

instructions: Squeeze all moisture from the grated onion using a cloth — wet onion prevents the mixture from binding.
Combine lamb mince with all ingredients except oil. Mix vigorously for 2 minutes until it becomes sticky and holds shape.
Refrigerate for 30 minutes to firm up.
Divide into 8 portions. With wet hands, press each portion around a flat metal skewer into a sausage shape, about 10cm long.
Brush lightly with oil.
Cook over high heat — grill, broiler, or cast iron — turning every 2–3 minutes until charred in places, about 12–15 minutes total.
Rest for 2 minutes.
Serve with mint chutney, sliced onion rings, and lemon wedges on naan or roomali roti.
tips: Squeeze every drop of moisture from the onion — this is the single most important step. If the mixture still won't hold, add 1 tbsp roasted chickpea flour (besan) as a binder. Fatty mince (20%) is essential for juicy kebabs.

name: Rasmalai
description: Bengal's most ethereal dessert — feather-light cottage cheese discs soaked in saffron-scented sweetened milk until swollen with flavor. Topped with rose water, pistachios, and dried rose petals, it is the jewel of Indian mithai.
instructions: Boil 1 litre milk. Remove from heat, add lemon juice gradually while stirring until milk curdles completely.
Strain through muslin cloth, rinse the curd under cold water to remove lemon taste. Hang for 30 minutes to drain.
Knead the chenna (cottage cheese) on a flat surface for 8–10 minutes until smooth and completely lump-free.
Divide into 12 equal portions and shape into flat discs.
Make a sugar syrup with 1 cup sugar and 4 cups water. Bring to a rolling boil.
Drop chenna discs into boiling syrup in batches of 4–5. Cover and cook on high heat for 15 minutes — they will puff up to nearly double in size.
While discs cook, simmer remaining 1 litre milk with 2 tbsp sugar, saffron, and cardamom, stirring frequently, until it reduces by half into a creamy rabri.
Remove chenna discs from syrup, gently press between palms to squeeze out excess syrup, then transfer to the rabri.
Add rose water, cool to room temperature, then refrigerate for at least 2 hours.
Serve chilled, garnished with sliced pistachios.
tips: Kneading the chenna is non-negotiable — under-kneaded curd gives grainy rasmalai. Knead until it feels like soft playdough. Always cook in a rolling boil (not a simmer) for light, spongy discs.

name: Maharashtrian Misal Pav
description: Pune's beloved street food — sprouted moth beans in a ferociously spiced curry topped with crunchy farsan, raw onion, tomato, coriander, and lemon, served with soft buttered pav buns. Maharashtra's most energetic dish.
instructions: Pressure cook sprouted moth beans with water and turmeric for 2 whistles until just cooked but still holding shape.
Dry roast coconut until golden, then blend with garlic, ginger, and a little water into a paste.
Heat oil in a pan. Add mustard seeds and curry leaves. When they splutter, add onions and fry until golden.
Add tomatoes and cook until soft. Add the coconut-ginger-garlic paste and cook 5 minutes.
Add chili powder, coriander powder, garam masala, and salt. Fry the masala for 3 minutes.
Add cooked moth beans and 1 cup water. Simmer covered for 15 minutes until gravy thickens.
Split and butter the pav buns, toast on a hot pan until golden.
To serve: ladle hot curry into bowls, pile farsan on top, then chopped onion, tomato, coriander, and a squeeze of lemon.
Serve immediately with toasted pav — the farsan must stay crunchy.
tips: Sprouted (not just soaked) moth beans are essential — they have a nuttier flavor and hold their shape better in the curry. Add farsan only at the table, never in the pot, to keep the crunch.

name: Khaman Dhokla
description: Gujarat's most celebrated snack — a steamed, spongy, golden savory cake of fermented chickpea batter, tempered with mustard seeds, green chili, and curry leaves, finished with fresh coconut and coriander. Impossibly light and addictive.
instructions: Mix chickpea flour, yogurt, water, lemon juice, ginger, green chili, turmeric, sugar, oil, and salt into a smooth, lump-free batter.
Grease a round steaming tin with oil.
Add ENO fruit salt to the batter and quickly stir until foamy — do not over-mix.
Immediately pour into the greased tin and place in a steamer.
Steam on high heat for 18–20 minutes until a toothpick inserted in the center comes out clean.
Cool for 5 minutes, then cut into diamond shapes.
For tempering: heat oil in a small pan, add mustard seeds. When they pop, add sesame seeds and curry leaves.
Pour tempering evenly over the dhokla.
Sprinkle with fresh coriander and grated coconut.
Serve with green chutney and tamarind chutney.
tips: Add ENO salt only when you're ready to pour into the steamer — once it activates, you have 30 seconds. Do not open the steamer lid during the first 15 minutes. For extra sponge, add 1 tsp sugar to the tempering oil.

name: Phirni
description: A Mughal delicacy from the royal kitchens — ground basmati rice simmered in full-fat milk with saffron, cardamom, and rose water until it becomes a velvety thick custard. Set in small earthenware bowls and chilled until trembling and fragrant.

ingredient_quantities.basmati rice: 3 tbsp, soaked 1 hour and coarsely ground
ingredient_quantities.saffron: a pinch, dissolved in warm milk

instructions: Soak basmati rice for 1 hour, drain, and grind coarsely — some texture is important, not a fine powder.
Bring milk to a boil in a heavy pot, then simmer on medium heat for 10 minutes to reduce slightly.
Mix ground rice with ½ cup cold milk until smooth, then pour into simmering milk while stirring constantly.
Cook on medium-low heat, stirring continuously, for 20 minutes until it thickens to a custard consistency.
Add sugar and stir for 3 more minutes until dissolved.
Remove from heat. Add cardamom powder, saffron milk, and rose water. Mix well.
Pour into small earthenware bowls or ramekins.
Cool to room temperature, then refrigerate for at least 4 hours until fully set.
Garnish with pistachios and almonds before serving.
Serve chilled.
tips: Stir continuously without pause — any break will cause the phirni to stick and scorch on the bottom. Clay bowls absorb a little moisture and give phirni its characteristic firm top layer; glass bowls give a softer set.
